text: Welcome to This is My Architecture. I am Han and today I am joined by Satish from Biopharma. Welcome.
Thank you, Han.
Before we get into your architecture, can you tell me a little bit about Biopharma?
Yeah. Our company is in the Biopharma industry. We develop targeted personalized treatments in the area of neuro disorders.
By neuro disorders, I mean diseases like Alzheimer's, schizophrenia, and Parkinson's.
So I see that you have a data lake. So once the data lake has been populated, how do you orchestrate and curate it?
Yeah. That's a great question. So once the data lake has all the data, we need to get the data to the processing layer.
So the first thing what we do is for MRI data processing, which we are discussing right now for this architecture,
is we have the AWS Batch Service, which acts as orchestrated. So when we create, when we run a job with an AWS Batch,
it creates many EC2 instances. In this case, we are talking hundreds to a few thousands of EC2 instances.
So the compute layer is instantiated. And the compute layer requires a storage for which we are using FSx.
And FSx is the one which obtains the data from the data lake, in this case.
So then this is enabled at the time of provisioning the FSx storage.
It's at the time of provisioning, we have the capability to sync with a particular input S3 folder.
And AWS Batch orchestrates this download and it reads the data. And all,
and this entire FSx storage is mounted onto an EC2 instance. This is where the processing happens
and the curation also happens during the time of processing.
So as a batch process, there must be a long processes that you may have quite a bit of errors.
How do you manage that?
Yeah. At the time of processing, definitely this is a multi-day job for most of it.
Depending upon the data sets, it can take anywhere from 24 days to seven days.
So at the time of processing, if an error has happened, we identify it this way.
We actually have AWS Batch sending events into EventBridge, even by notifying the change in status of AWS Batch.
That's one. These are the events that are communicated.
These events are being sent by NAMDA onto anastic search where they get indexed.
That's how we get to know anything that's failing. We immediately get to know of the events through anastic search.
So from the data science perspective, how do they visualize and consume the data sets?
Yeah. Our data scientists and bio-nagists are the primary consumers of this pipeline.
Okay.
So how do they come to know us? We have all the logs being sent from AWS Batch into anastic search.
That's one. They can actually look into the logs to see if they are more technical.
They would like to know what on subjects have been processed.
So that information is communicated, again, from AWS Batch to NAMDA, NAMDA indexes.
That's one. And the second thing is the output that's produced during the processing.
The output that's produced, that's produced is actually stored in FSX, which is again synced into data make using an activity called a DRT or a data repository task, which is provided by AWS.
This automatically syncs terabytes of data that's produced on FSX back into the data make.
And this data is someone that contains all the required coordination matrices required for visualizing this data.
So our bio-nagists can look into the data make to see the data.

Awesome. So it sounds like you're able to not only ingest and curate and orchestrate the data, you're able to handle the errors and process it where the data scientists can visualize the data and also see the status from the long way to the data.
So that they can see the petabytes of data that you have for the MRI scans to help with that accelerated processing.
Exactly. So it all helps.
One of the things that we do is help our bio-nagists and data scientists take advantage of the pipeline.

So they are going to trigger the pipeline and they can monitor the entire pipeline with themselves and also look at the visualizations that are also enabled by anastic search or the Kibana dashboards are being exposed to visualize.
And we also have the data being visualized in our data warehouse that's powered by data make.
So thank you so much for going over your architecture.

That is amazing how you're able to help the data scientists not only visualize the data, but also help with that rapid accelerated research and development for personalized neuro disease and also drug discovery as well.
So thank you so much.
Thank you so much, Han.
Thank you for watching. This is my architecture.
